'''Sadakichi Kita''' (喜田 貞吉, July 11, 1871 – July 3, 1939) was a pre-World War II Japanese historian and literary scholar. He integrated [[:en:Archaeology|archaeology]] and [[:en:Folklore studies|folklore studies]] into his academic research.
== Career ==
Sadakichi Kita was born into a farming family in {{ill|Kushibuchi Village, Awa Province|ja|阿波国那賀郡櫛淵村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kushibuchi Village, Awa Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (present-day {{ill|Komatsushima City, Tokushima Prefecture|ja|徳島県小松島市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Komatsushima City, Tokushima Prefecture|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}). After graduating from local schools, he advanced to {{ill|Tokushima Junior High School|ja|徳島中学校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokushima Junior High School|1=Tokushima Junior High School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q23996561}}, followed by [[:en:Third Higher School|Third High School (Old System)]]. In 1893, at the age of 23, he enrolled in the Faculty of Letters at [[:en:Imperial Universities|Tokyo Imperial University]], where he studied history. Among his classmates were {{ill|Ginzō Uchida|ja|内田銀蔵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ginzō Uchida|1=Ginzō Uchida|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11394369}} and {{ill|Katsumi Kuroita|ja|黒板勝美|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsumi Kuroita|1=Katsumi Kuroita|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3894478}}. After graduating in 1896 from the Department of National History, he continued his postgraduate studies under scholars such as {{ill|Kumazo Tsuboi|ja|坪井九馬三|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumazo Tsuboi|1=Tsuboi Kumezō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11425688}}, focusing on "the historical geography of Japan."
During his time as a lecturer at the university, Kita published textbooks such as "Japanese Geography" and "Foreign Geography" (co-authored with {{ill|Shigetomo Kōda|ja|幸田成友|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shigetomo Kōda|1=Shigetomo Kōda|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q21699628}}). In 1909, he earned a {{ill|Doctorate in Literature|ja|文学博士|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Doctorate in Literature|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 博士（文学）|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 博士（文学）|1=Doctor of Letters|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q837343}} for his research on [[:en:Heijō-kyō|Heijō-kyō]] and the {{ill|Reconstruction debate of Hōryū-ji|ja|法隆寺再建論争|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Reconstruction debate of Hōryū-ji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Later, his involvement in writing {{ill|national textbooks|ja|国定教科書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/national textbooks|1=national textbooks|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420710}} for elementary schools led to controversy. In 1911, his decision to present both the [[:en:Northern Court|Northern Court]] and {{ill|Southern Court|ja|南朝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Southern Court|qq=}} during the [[:en:Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|Nanboku-chō period]] in textbooks resulted in public backlash, eventually leading to his temporary suspension.
From 1913, Kita served as a lecturer at {{ill|Kyoto Imperial University|ja|京都帝国大学|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto Imperial University|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 京都大学|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 京都大学|1=Kyoto University|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q336264}} and became a professor there in 1920. By 1924, he had joined {{ill|Tohoku Imperial University|ja|東北帝国大学|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tohoku Imperial University|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東北大学|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東北大学|1=Tohoku University|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1062129}}. There, he laid the foundation for the study of ancient Japanese history and archaeology.
== Contributions to Research ==
Kita proposed independent theories on the formation of the Japanese ethnic group and explored the history of Japan's origins through archaeological and historical studies. One notable hypothesis suggested that people creating [[:en:Jōmon pottery|Jōmon pottery]] continued to live in Eastern Japan until the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], sparking the {{ill|Minerva Debate|ja|ミネルヴァ論争|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minerva Debate|1=Minerva Debate|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109358929}}.
== Works == === Major Publications === * ''Japanese Geography'' (1899) * ''Hōryū-ji and Its Reconstruction Debate'' (1910) * ''Stories of Japanese History'' (1928) * ''Studies on Buraku Issues'' (1968)
== Legacy == Kita's pioneering studies bridged history and archaeology, influencing subsequent generations of scholars. His work on Japanese folklore and geographical history remains integral to the understanding of Japan's cultural heritage.
== References == * {{Cite book|author=Eiji Oguma|title=The Myth of a Homogeneous Nation|year=1995}} * {{Cite journal|author=Daisuke Itō|title=Archival Materials on Sadakichi Kita|journal=Tohoku Asian Studies}}
== External Links == * [http://www.example.com Biography of Sadakichi Kita]

'''Sadakichi Kita''' (喜田 貞吉, July 11, 1871 – July 3, 1939) was a pre-World War II Japanese historian and literary scholar. He integrated [[:en:Archaeology|archaeology]] and [[:en:Folklore studies|folklore studies]] into his academic research.
== Career ==
Sadakichi Kita was born into a farming family in {{ill|Kushibuchi Village, Awa Province|ja|阿波国那賀郡櫛淵村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kushibuchi Village, Awa Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (present-day {{ill|Komatsushima City, Tokushima Prefecture|ja|徳島県小松島市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Komatsushima City, Tokushima Prefecture|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}). After graduating from local schools, he advanced to {{ill|Tokushima Junior High School|ja|徳島中学校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokushima Junior High School|1=Tokushima Junior High School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q23996561}}, followed by [[:en:Third Higher School|Third High School (Old System)]]. In 1893, at the age of 23, he enrolled in the Faculty of Letters at [[:en:Imperial Universities|Tokyo Imperial University]], where he studied history. Among his classmates were {{ill|Ginzō Uchida|ja|内田銀蔵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ginzō Uchida|1=Ginzō Uchida|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11394369}} and {{ill|Katsumi Kuroita|ja|黒板勝美|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsumi Kuroita|1=Katsumi Kuroita|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3894478}}. After graduating in 1896 from the Department of National History, he continued his postgraduate studies under scholars such as {{ill|Kumazo Tsuboi|ja|坪井九馬三|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumazo Tsuboi|1=Tsuboi Kumezō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11425688}}, focusing on "the historical geography of Japan."
During his time as a lecturer at the university, Kita published textbooks such as "Japanese Geography" and "Foreign Geography" (co-authored with {{ill|Shigetomo Kōda|ja|幸田成友|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shigetomo Kōda|1=Shigetomo Kōda|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q21699628}}). In 1909, he earned a {{ill|Doctorate in Literature|ja|文学博士|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Doctorate in Literature|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 博士（文学）|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 博士（文学）|1=Doctor of Letters|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q837343}} for his research on [[:en:Heijō-kyō|Heijō-kyō]] and the {{ill|Reconstruction debate of Hōryū-ji|ja|法隆寺再建論争|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Reconstruction debate of Hōryū-ji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Later, his involvement in writing {{ill|national textbooks|ja|国定教科書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/national textbooks|1=national textbooks|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420710}} for elementary schools led to controversy. In 1911, his decision to present both the [[:en:Northern Court|Northern Court]] and {{ill|Southern Court|ja|南朝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Southern Court|qq=}} during the [[:en:Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|Nanboku-chō period]] in textbooks resulted in public backlash, eventually leading to his temporary suspension.
From 1913, Kita served as a lecturer at {{ill|Kyoto Imperial University|ja|京都帝国大学|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto Imperial University|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 京都大学|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 京都大学|1=Kyoto University|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q336264}} and became a professor there in 1920. By 1924, he had joined {{ill|Tohoku Imperial University|ja|東北帝国大学|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tohoku Imperial University|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東北大学|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東北大学|1=Tohoku University|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1062129}}. There, he laid the foundation for the study of ancient Japanese history and archaeology.
== Contributions to Research ==
Kita proposed independent theories on the formation of the Japanese ethnic group and explored the history of Japan's origins through archaeological and historical studies. One notable hypothesis suggested that people creating [[:en:Jōmon pottery|Jōmon pottery]] continued to live in Eastern Japan until the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], sparking the {{ill|Minerva Debate|ja|ミネルヴァ論争|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minerva Debate|1=Minerva Debate|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109358929}}.
== Works == === Major Publications === * ''Japanese Geography'' (1899) * ''Hōryū-ji and Its Reconstruction Debate'' (1910) * ''Stories of Japanese History'' (1928) * ''Studies on Buraku Issues'' (1968)
== Legacy == Kita's pioneering studies bridged history and archaeology, influencing subsequent generations of scholars. His work on Japanese folklore and geographical history remains integral to the understanding of Japan's cultural heritage.
== References == * {{Cite book|author=Eiji Oguma|title=The Myth of a Homogeneous Nation|year=1995}} * {{Cite journal|author=Daisuke Itō|title=Archival Materials on Sadakichi Kita|journal=Tohoku Asian Studies}}
== External Links == * [http://www.example.com Biography of Sadakichi Kita]

Tokyo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q7842}}, {{ill|Kyoto Imperial University|ja|京都帝国大学|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto Imperial University|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to Imperial University|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東北大学|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東北大学|1=Tohoku University|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1062129}} |known_for = Proposing numerous hypotheses on the formation of the Japanese ethnic group through the lenses of history and archaeology }}
'''Sadakichi Kita''' (喜田 貞吉, July 11, 1871 – July 3, 1939) was a pre-World War II Japanese historian and literary scholar. He integrated [[:en:Archaeology|archaeology]] and [[:en:Folklore studies|folklore studies]] into his academic research.
== Career ==
Sadakichi Kita was born into a farming family in {{ill|Kushibuchi Village, Awa Province|ja|阿波国那賀郡櫛淵村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kushibuchi Village, Awa Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (present-day {{ill|Komatsushima City, Tokushima Prefecture|ja|徳島県小松島市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Komatsushima City, Tokushima Prefecture|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}). After graduating from local schools, he advanced to {{ill|Tokushima Junior High School|ja|徳島中学校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokushima Junior High School|1=Tokushima Junior High School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q23996561}}, followed by [[:en:Third Higher School|Third High School (Old System)]]. In 1893, at the age of 23, he enrolled in the Faculty of Letters at [[:en:Imperial Universities|Tokyo Imperial University]], where he studied history. Among his classmates were {{ill|Ginzō Uchida|ja|内田銀蔵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ginzō Uchida|1=Ginzō Uchida|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11394369}} and {{ill|Katsumi Kuroita|ja|黒板勝美|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsumi Kuroita|1=Katsumi Kuroita|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3894478}}. After graduating in 1896 from the Department of National History, he continued his postgraduate studies under scholars such as {{ill|Kumazo Tsuboi|ja|坪井九馬三|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumazo Tsuboi|1=Tsuboi Kumezō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11425688}}, focusing on "the historical geography of Japan."
During his time as a lecturer at the university, Kita published textbooks such as "Japanese Geography" and "Foreign Geography" (co-authored with {{ill|Shigetomo Kōda|ja|幸田成友|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shigetomo Kōda|1=Shigetomo Kōda|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q21699628}}). In 1909, he earned a {{ill|Doctorate in Literature|ja|文学博士|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Doctorate in Literature|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 博士（文学）|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 博士（文学）|1=Doctor of Letters|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q837343}} for his research on [[:en:Heijō-kyō|Heijō-kyō]] and the {{ill|Reconstruction debate of Hōryū-ji|ja|法隆寺再建論争|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Reconstruction debate of Hōryū-ji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Later, his involvement in writing {{ill|national textbooks|ja|国定教科書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/national textbooks|1=national textbooks|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420710}} for elementary schools led to controversy. In 1911, his decision to present both the [[:en:Northern Court|Northern Court]] and {{ill|Southern Court|ja|南朝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Southern Court|qq=}} during the [[:en:Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|Nanboku-chō period]] in textbooks resulted in public backlash, eventually leading to his temporary suspension.
From 1913, Kita served as a lecturer at {{ill|Kyoto Imperial University|ja|京都帝国大学|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto Imperial University|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 京都大学|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 京都大学|1=Kyoto University|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q336264}} and became a professor there in 1920. By 1924, he had joined {{ill|Tohoku Imperial University|ja|東北帝国大学|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tohoku Imperial University|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東北大学|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東北大学|1=Tohoku University|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1062129}}. There, he laid the foundation for the study of ancient Japanese history and archaeology.
== Contributions to Research ==
Kita proposed independent theories on the formation of the Japanese ethnic group and explored the history of Japan's origins through archaeological and historical studies. One notable hypothesis suggested that people creating [[:en:Jōmon pottery|Jōmon pottery]] continued to live in Eastern Japan until the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], sparking the {{ill|Minerva Debate|ja|ミネルヴァ論争|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minerva Debate|1=Minerva Debate|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109358929}}.
== Works == === Major Publications === * ''Japanese Geography'' (1899) * ''Hōryū-ji and Its Reconstruction Debate'' (1910) * ''Stories of Japanese History'' (1928) * ''Studies on Buraku Issues'' (1968)
== Legacy == Kita's pioneering studies bridged history and archaeology, influencing subsequent generations of scholars. His work on Japanese folklore and geographical history remains integral to the understanding of Japan's cultural heritage.
== References == * {{Cite book|author=Eiji Oguma|title=The Myth of a Homogeneous Nation|year=1995}} * {{Cite journal|author=Daisuke Itō|title=Archival Materials on Sadakichi Kita|journal=Tohoku Asian Studies}}
== External Links == * [http://www.example.com Biography of Sadakichi Kita]

Among his classmates were {{ill|Ginzō Uchida|ja|内田銀蔵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ginzō Uchida|1=Ginzō Uchida|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11394369}} and {{ill|Katsumi Kuroita|ja|黒板勝美|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsumi Kuroita|1=Katsumi Kuroita|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3894478}}. After graduating in 1896 from the Department of National History, he continued his postgraduate studies under scholars such as {{ill|Kumazo Tsuboi|ja|坪井九馬三|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumazo Tsuboi|1=Tsuboi Kumezō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11425688}}, focusing on "the historical geography of Japan."
During his time as a lecturer at the university, Kita published textbooks such as "Japanese Geography" and "Foreign Geography" (co-authored with {{ill|Shigetomo Kōda|ja|幸田成友|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shigetomo Kōda|1=Shigetomo Kōda|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q21699628}}). In 1909, he earned a {{ill|Doctorate in Literature|ja|文学博士|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Doctorate in Literature|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 博士（文学）|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 博士（文学）|1=Doctor of Letters|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q837343}} for his research on [[:en:Heijō-kyō|Heijō-kyō]] and the {{ill|Reconstruction debate of Hōryū-ji|ja|法隆寺再建論争|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Reconstruction debate of Hōryū-ji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Later, his involvement in writing {{ill|national textbooks|ja|国定教科書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/national textbooks|1=national textbooks|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420710}} for elementary schools led to controversy. In 1911, his decision to present both the [[:en:Northern Court|Northern Court]] and {{ill|Southern Court|ja|南朝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Southern Court|qq=}} during the [[:en:Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|Nanboku-chō period]] in textbooks resulted in public backlash, eventually leading to his temporary suspension.
From 1913, Kita served as a lecturer at {{ill|Kyoto Imperial University|ja|京都帝国大学|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto Imperial University|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 京都大学|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 京都大学|1=Kyoto University|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q336264}} and became a professor there in 1920. By 1924, he had joined {{ill|Tohoku Imperial University|ja|東北帝国大学|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tohoku Imperial University|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東北大学|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東北大学|1=Tohoku University|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1062129}}. There, he laid the foundation for the study of ancient Japanese history and archaeology.
== Contributions to Research ==
Kita proposed independent theories on the formation of the Japanese ethnic group and explored the history of Japan's origins through archaeological and historical studies. One notable hypothesis suggested that people creating [[:en:Jōmon pottery|Jōmon pottery]] continued to live in Eastern Japan until the [[:en:Kamakura period|Kamakura period]], sparking the {{ill|Minerva Debate|ja|ミネルヴァ論争|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minerva Debate|1=Minerva Debate|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109358929}}.
== Works == === Major Publications === * ''Japanese Geography'' (1899) * ''Hōryū-ji and Its Reconstruction Debate'' (1910) * ''Stories of Japanese History'' (1928) * ''Studies on Buraku Issues'' (1968)
== Legacy == Kita's pioneering studies bridged history and archaeology, influencing subsequent generations of scholars. His work on Japanese folklore and geographical history remains integral to the understanding of Japan's cultural heritage.
== References == * {{Cite book|author=Eiji Oguma|title=The Myth of a Homogeneous Nation|year=1995}} * {{Cite journal|author=Daisuke Itō|title=Archival Materials on Sadakichi Kita|journal=Tohoku Asian Studies}}
== External Links == * [http://www.example.com Biography of Sadakichi Kita]

'''Sadakichi Kita''' (喜田 貞吉, July 11, 1871 – July 3, 1939) was a pre-World War II Japanese historian and literary scholar. He integrated {{ill|Archaeology|en|Archaeology|WD=Q23498|lt=archaeology}} and {{ill|Folklore studies|en|Folklore studies|WD=Q132116|lt=folklore studies}} into his academic research.
== Career ==
Sadakichi Kita was born into a farming family in {{ill|Kushibuchi Village, Awa Province|ja|阿波国那賀郡櫛淵村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kushibuchi Village, Awa Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (present-day {{ill|Komatsushima City, Tokushima Prefecture|ja|徳島県小松島市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Komatsushima City, Tokushima Prefecture|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}). After graduating from local schools, he advanced to {{ill|Tokushima Junior High School|ja|徳島中学校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokushima Junior High School|1=Tokushima Junior High School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q23996561}}, followed by {{ill|Third Higher School|en|Third Higher School|WD=Q11602219|lt=Third High School (Old System)}}. In 1893, at the age of 23, he enrolled in the Faculty of Letters at {{ill|Imperial Universities|en|Imperial Universities|WD=Q562092|lt=Tokyo Imperial University}}, where he studied history. Among his classmates were {{ill|Ginzō Uchida|ja|内田銀蔵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ginzō Uchida|1=Ginzō Uchida|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11394369}} and {{ill|Katsumi Kuroita|ja|黒板勝美|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsumi Kuroita|1=Katsumi Kuroita|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3894478}}. After graduating in 1896 from the Department of National History, he continued his postgraduate studies under scholars such as {{ill|Kumazo Tsuboi|ja|坪井九馬三|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumazo Tsuboi|1=Tsuboi Kumezō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11425688}}, focusing on "the historical geography of Japan."
During his time as a lecturer at the university, Kita published textbooks such as "Japanese Geography" and "Foreign Geography" (co-authored with {{ill|Shigetomo Kōda|ja|幸田成友|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shigetomo Kōda|1=Shigetomo Kōda|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q21699628}}). In 1909, he earned a {{ill|Doctorate in Literature|ja|文学博士|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Doctorate in Literature|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 博士（文学）|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 博士（文学）|1=Doctor of Letters|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q837343}} for his research on {{ill|Heijō-kyō|en|Heijō-kyō|WD=Q862509|lt=Heijō-kyō}} and the {{ill|Reconstruction debate of Hōryū-ji|ja|法隆寺再建論争|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Reconstruction debate of Hōryū-ji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Later, his involvement in writing {{ill|national textbooks|ja|国定教科書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/national textbooks|1=national textbooks|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420710}} for elementary schools led to controversy. In 1911, his decision to present both the {{ill|Northern Court|en|Northern Court|WD=Q1201819|lt=Northern Court}} and {{ill|Southern Court|ja|南朝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Southern Court|qq=}} during the {{ill|Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|en|Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|WD=Q8140349|lt=Nanboku-chō period}} in textbooks resulted in public backlash, eventually leading to his temporary suspension.
From 1913, Kita served as a lecturer at {{ill|Kyoto Imperial University|ja|京都帝国大学|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto Imperial University|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 京都大学|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 京都大学|1=Kyoto University|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q336264}} and became a professor there in 1920. By 1924, he had joined {{ill|Tohoku Imperial University|ja|東北帝国大学|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tohoku Imperial University|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東北大学|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東北大学|1=Tohoku University|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1062129}}. There, he laid the foundation for the study of ancient Japanese history and archaeology.
== Contributions to Research ==
Kita proposed independent theories on the formation of the Japanese ethnic group and explored the history of Japan's origins through archaeological and historical studies. One notable hypothesis suggested that people creating {{ill|Jōmon pottery|en|Jōmon pottery|WD=Q4390366|lt=Jōmon pottery}} continued to live in Eastern Japan until the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}}, sparking the {{ill|Minerva Debate|ja|ミネルヴァ論争|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minerva Debate|1=Minerva Debate|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109358929}}.
== Works == === Major Publications === * ''Japanese Geography'' (1899) * ''Hōryū-ji and Its Reconstruction Debate'' (1910) * ''Stories of Japanese History'' (1928) * ''Studies on Buraku Issues'' (1968)
== Legacy == Kita's pioneering studies bridged history and archaeology, influencing subsequent generations of scholars. His work on Japanese folklore and geographical history remains integral to the understanding of Japan's cultural heritage.
== References == * {{Cite book|author=Eiji Oguma|title=The Myth of a Homogeneous Nation|year=1995}} * {{Cite journal|author=Daisuke Itō|title=Archival Materials on Sadakichi Kita|journal=Tohoku Asian Studies}}

In 1911, his decision to present both the {{ill|Northern Court|en|Northern Court|WD=Q1201819|lt=Northern Court}} and {{ill|Southern Court|ja|南朝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Southern Court|qq=}} during the {{ill|Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|en|Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|WD=Q8140349|lt=Nanboku-chō period}} in textbooks resulted in public backlash, eventually leading to his temporary suspension.
From 1913, Kita served as a lecturer at {{ill|Kyoto Imperial University|ja|京都帝国大学|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto Imperial University|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 京都大学|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 京都大学|1=Kyoto University|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q336264}} and became a professor there in 1920. By 1924, he had joined {{ill|Tohoku Imperial University|ja|東北帝国大学|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tohoku Imperial University|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東北大学|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東北大学|1=Tohoku University|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1062129}}. There, he laid the foundation for the study of ancient Japanese history and archaeology.
== Contributions to Research ==
Kita proposed independent theories on the formation of the Japanese ethnic group and explored the history of Japan's origins through archaeological and historical studies. One notable hypothesis suggested that people creating {{ill|Jōmon pottery|en|Jōmon pottery|WD=Q4390366|lt=Jōmon pottery}} continued to live in Eastern Japan until the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}}, sparking the {{ill|Minerva Debate|ja|ミネルヴァ論争|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minerva Debate|1=Minerva Debate|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109358929}}.
== Works == === Major Publications === * ''Japanese Geography'' (1899) * ''Hōryū-ji and Its Reconstruction Debate'' (1910) * ''Stories of Japanese History'' (1928) * ''Studies on Buraku Issues'' (1968)
== Legacy == Kita's pioneering studies bridged history and archaeology, influencing subsequent generations of scholars. His work on Japanese folklore and geographical history remains integral to the understanding of Japan's cultural heritage.

'''Sadakichi Kita''' (喜田 貞吉, July 11, 1871 – July 3, 1939) was a pre-World War II Japanese historian and literary scholar. He integrated {{ill|Archaeology|en|Archaeology|WD=Q23498|lt=archaeology}} and {{ill|Folklore studies|en|Folklore studies|WD=Q132116|lt=folklore studies}} into his academic research.
== Career ==
Sadakichi Kita was born into a farming family in {{ill|Kushibuchi Village, Awa Province|ja|阿波国那賀郡櫛淵村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kushibuchi Village, Awa Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (present-day {{ill|Komatsushima City, Tokushima Prefecture|ja|徳島県小松島市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Komatsushima City, Tokushima Prefecture|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}). After graduating from local schools, he advanced to {{ill|Tokushima Junior High School|ja|徳島中学校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokushima Junior High School|1=Tokushima Junior High School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q23996561}}, followed by {{ill|Third Higher School|en|Third Higher School|WD=Q11602219|lt=Third High School (Old System)}}. In 1893, at the age of 23, he enrolled in the Faculty of Letters at {{ill|Imperial Universities|en|Imperial Universities|WD=Q562092|lt=Tokyo Imperial University}}, where he studied history. Among his classmates were {{ill|Ginzō Uchida|ja|内田銀蔵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ginzō Uchida|1=Ginzō Uchida|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11394369}} and {{ill|Katsumi Kuroita|ja|黒板勝美|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsumi Kuroita|1=Katsumi Kuroita|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3894478}}. After graduating in 1896 from the Department of National History, he continued his postgraduate studies under scholars such as {{ill|Kumazo Tsuboi|ja|坪井九馬三|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumazo Tsuboi|1=Tsuboi Kumezō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11425688}}, focusing on "the historical geography of Japan."
During his time as a lecturer at the university, Kita published textbooks such as "Japanese Geography" and "Foreign Geography" (co-authored with {{ill|Shigetomo Kōda|ja|幸田成友|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shigetomo Kōda|1=Shigetomo Kōda|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q21699628}}). In 1909, he earned a {{ill|Doctorate in Literature|ja|文学博士|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Doctorate in Literature|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 博士（文学）|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 博士（文学）|1=Doctor of Letters|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q837343}} for his research on {{ill|Heijō-kyō|en|Heijō-kyō|WD=Q862509|lt=Heijō-kyō}} and the {{ill|Reconstruction debate of Hōryū-ji|ja|法隆寺再建論争|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Reconstruction debate of Hōryū-ji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Later, his involvement in writing {{ill|national textbooks|ja|国定教科書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/national textbooks|1=national textbooks|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420710}} for elementary schools led to controversy. In 1911, his decision to present both the {{ill|Northern Court|en|Northern Court|WD=Q1201819|lt=Northern Court}} and {{ill|Southern Court|ja|南朝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Southern Court|qq=}} during the {{ill|Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|en|Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|WD=Q8140349|lt=Nanboku-chō period}} in textbooks resulted in public backlash, eventually leading to his temporary suspension.
From 1913, Kita served as a lecturer at {{ill|Kyoto Imperial University|ja|京都帝国大学|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto Imperial University|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 京都大学|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 京都大学|1=Kyoto University|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q336264}} and became a professor there in 1920. By 1924, he had joined {{ill|Tohoku Imperial University|ja|東北帝国大学|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tohoku Imperial University|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東北大学|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東北大学|1=Tohoku University|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1062129}}. There, he laid the foundation for the study of ancient Japanese history and archaeology.
== Contributions to Research ==
Kita proposed independent theories on the formation of the Japanese ethnic group and explored the history of Japan's origins through archaeological and historical studies. One notable hypothesis suggested that people creating {{ill|Jōmon pottery|en|Jōmon pottery|WD=Q4390366|lt=Jōmon pottery}} continued to live in Eastern Japan until the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}}, sparking the {{ill|Minerva Debate|ja|ミネルヴァ論争|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minerva Debate|1=Minerva Debate|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109358929}}.
== Works == === Major Publications === * ''Japanese Geography'' (1899) * ''Hōryū-ji and Its Reconstruction Debate'' (1910) * ''Stories of Japanese History'' (1928) * ''Studies on Buraku Issues'' (1968)
== Legacy == Kita's pioneering studies bridged history and archaeology, influencing subsequent generations of scholars. His work on Japanese folklore and geographical history remains integral to the understanding of Japan's cultural heritage.
== References == * {{Cite book|author=Eiji Oguma|title=The Myth of a Homogeneous Nation|year=1995}} * {{Cite journal|author=Daisuke Itō|title=Archival Materials on Sadakichi Kita|journal=Tohoku Asian Studies}}

(旧暦)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/May 24 (lunar calendar)|1=24th day of the 5th month in the Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q839627}}) |birth_place = {{JPN}} University|ja|東北帝国大学|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tohoku Imperial University|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東北大学|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東北大学|1=Tohoku University|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1062129}} |known_for = Proposing numerous hypotheses on the formation of the Japanese ethnic group through the lenses of history and archaeology }}
'''Sadakichi Kita''' (喜田 貞吉, July 11, 1871 – July 3, 1939) was a pre-World War II Japanese historian and literary scholar. He integrated {{ill|Archaeology|en|Archaeology|WD=Q23498|lt=archaeology}} and {{ill|Folklore studies|en|Folklore studies|WD=Q132116|lt=folklore studies}} into his academic research.
== Career ==
Sadakichi Kita was born into a farming family in {{ill|Kushibuchi Village, Awa Province|ja|阿波国那賀郡櫛淵村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kushibuchi Village, Awa Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (present-day {{ill|Komatsushima City, Tokushima Prefecture|ja|徳島県小松島市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Komatsushima City, Tokushima Prefecture|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}). After graduating from local schools, he advanced to {{ill|Tokushima Junior High School|ja|徳島中学校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokushima Junior High School|1=Tokushima Junior High School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q23996561}}, followed by {{ill|Third Higher School|en|Third Higher School|WD=Q11602219|lt=Third High School (Old System)}}. In 1893, at the age of 23, he enrolled in the Faculty of Letters at {{ill|Imperial Universities|en|Imperial Universities|WD=Q562092|lt=Tokyo Imperial University}}, where he studied history. Among his classmates were {{ill|Ginzō Uchida|ja|内田銀蔵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ginzō Uchida|1=Ginzō Uchida|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11394369}} and {{ill|Katsumi Kuroita|ja|黒板勝美|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsumi Kuroita|1=Katsumi Kuroita|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3894478}}. After graduating in 1896 from the Department of National History, he continued his postgraduate studies under scholars such as {{ill|Kumazo Tsuboi|ja|坪井九馬三|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumazo Tsuboi|1=Tsuboi Kumezō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11425688}}, focusing on "the historical geography of Japan."
During his time as a lecturer at the university, Kita published textbooks such as "Japanese Geography" and "Foreign Geography" (co-authored with {{ill|Shigetomo Kōda|ja|幸田成友|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shigetomo Kōda|1=Shigetomo Kōda|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q21699628}}). In 1909, he earned a {{ill|Doctorate in Literature|ja|文学博士|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Doctorate in Literature|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 博士（文学）|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 博士（文学）|1=Doctor of Letters|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q837343}} for his research on {{ill|Heijō-kyō|en|Heijō-kyō|WD=Q862509|lt=Heijō-kyō}} and the {{ill|Reconstruction debate of Hōryū-ji|ja|法隆寺再建論争|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Reconstruction debate of Hōryū-ji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Later, his involvement in writing {{ill|national textbooks|ja|国定教科書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/national textbooks|1=national textbooks|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420710}} for elementary schools led to controversy. In 1911, his decision to present both the {{ill|Northern Court|en|Northern Court|WD=Q1201819|lt=Northern Court}} and {{ill|Southern Court|ja|南朝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Southern Court|qq=}} during the {{ill|Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|en|Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|WD=Q8140349|lt=Nanboku-chō period}} in textbooks resulted in public backlash, eventually leading to his temporary suspension.
From 1913, Kita served as a lecturer at {{ill|Kyoto Imperial University|ja|京都帝国大学|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto Imperial University|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 京都大学|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 京都大学|1=Kyoto University|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q336264}} and became a professor there in 1920. By 1924, he had joined {{ill|Tohoku Imperial University|ja|東北帝国大学|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tohoku Imperial University|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東北大学|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東北大学|1=Tohoku University|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1062129}}. There, he laid the foundation for the study of ancient Japanese history and archaeology.
== Contributions to Research ==
Kita proposed independent theories on the formation of the Japanese ethnic group and explored the history of Japan's origins through archaeological and historical studies. One notable hypothesis suggested that people creating {{ill|Jōmon pottery|en|Jōmon pottery|WD=Q4390366|lt=Jōmon pottery}} continued to live in Eastern Japan until the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}}, sparking the {{ill|Minerva Debate|ja|ミネルヴァ論争|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minerva Debate|1=Minerva Debate|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109358929}}.
== Works == === Major Publications === * ''Japanese Geography'' (1899) * ''Hōryū-ji and Its Reconstruction Debate'' (1910) * ''Stories of Japanese History'' (1928) * ''Studies on Buraku Issues'' (1968)
== Legacy == Kita's pioneering studies bridged history and archaeology, influencing subsequent generations of scholars. His work on Japanese folklore and geographical history remains integral to the understanding of Japan's cultural heritage.
== References == * {{Cite book|author=Eiji Oguma|title=The Myth of a Homogeneous Nation|year=1995}} * {{Cite journal|author=Daisuke Itō|title=Archival Materials on Sadakichi Kita|journal=Tohoku Asian Studies}}
== External Links == * [http://www.example.com Biography of Sadakichi Kita]

'''Sadakichi Kita''' (喜田 貞吉, July 11, 1871 – July 3, 1939) was a pre-World War II Japanese historian and literary scholar. He integrated {{ill|Archaeology|en|Archaeology|WD=Q23498|lt=archaeology|1=Archaeology}} and {{ill|Folklore studies|en|Folklore studies|WD=Q132116|lt=folklore studies|1=Folklore studies}} into his academic research.
== Career ==
Sadakichi Kita was born into a farming family in {{ill|Kushibuchi Village, Awa Province|ja|阿波国那賀郡櫛淵村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kushibuchi Village, Awa Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (present-day {{ill|Komatsushima City, Tokushima Prefecture|ja|徳島県小松島市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Komatsushima City, Tokushima Prefecture|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}). After graduating from local schools, he advanced to {{ill|Tokushima Junior High School|ja|徳島中学校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokushima Junior High School|1=Tokushima Junior High School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q23996561}}, followed by {{ill|Third Higher School|en|Third Higher School|WD=Q11602219|lt=Third High School (Old System)|1=Third Higher School}}. In 1893, at the age of 23, he enrolled in the Faculty of Letters at {{ill|Imperial Universities|en|Imperial Universities|WD=Q562092|lt=Tokyo Imperial University|1=Imperial Universities}}, where he studied history. Among his classmates were {{ill|Ginzō Uchida|ja|内田銀蔵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ginzō Uchida|1=Ginzō Uchida|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11394369}} and {{ill|Katsumi Kuroita|ja|黒板勝美|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsumi Kuroita|1=Katsumi Kuroita|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3894478}}. After graduating in 1896 from the Department of National History, he continued his postgraduate studies under scholars such as {{ill|Kumazo Tsuboi|ja|坪井九馬三|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumazo Tsuboi|1=Tsuboi Kumezō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11425688}}, focusing on "the historical geography of Japan."
During his time as a lecturer at the university, Kita published textbooks such as "Japanese Geography" and "Foreign Geography" (co-authored with {{ill|Shigetomo Kōda|ja|幸田成友|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shigetomo Kōda|1=Shigetomo Kōda|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q21699628}}). In 1909, he earned a {{ill|Doctorate in Literature|ja|文学博士|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Doctorate in Literature|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 博士（文学）|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 博士（文学）|1=Doctor of Letters|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q837343}} for his research on {{ill|Heijō-kyō|en|Heijō-kyō|WD=Q862509|lt=Heijō-kyō|1=Heijō-kyō}} and the {{ill|Reconstruction debate of Hōryū-ji|ja|法隆寺再建論争|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Reconstruction debate of Hōryū-ji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Later, his involvement in writing {{ill|national textbooks|ja|国定教科書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/national textbooks|1=national textbooks|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420710}} for elementary schools led to controversy. In 1911, his decision to present both the {{ill|Northern Court|en|Northern Court|WD=Q1201819|lt=Northern Court|1=Northern Court}} and {{ill|Southern Court|ja|南朝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Southern Court|qq=}} during the {{ill|Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|en|Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|WD=Q8140349|lt=Nanboku-chō period|1=Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)}} in textbooks resulted in public backlash, eventually leading to his temporary suspension.
From 1913, Kita served as a lecturer at {{ill|Kyoto Imperial University|ja|京都帝国大学|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto Imperial University|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 京都大学|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 京都大学|1=Kyoto University|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q336264}} and became a professor there in 1920. By 1924, he had joined {{ill|Tohoku Imperial University|ja|東北帝国大学|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tohoku Imperial University|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東北大学|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東北大学|1=Tohoku University|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1062129}}. There, he laid the foundation for the study of ancient Japanese history and archaeology.
== Contributions to Research ==
Kita proposed independent theories on the formation of the Japanese ethnic group and explored the history of Japan's origins through archaeological and historical studies. One notable hypothesis suggested that people creating {{ill|Jōmon pottery|en|Jōmon pottery|WD=Q4390366|lt=Jōmon pottery|1=Jōmon pottery}} continued to live in Eastern Japan until the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}}, sparking the {{ill|Minerva Debate|ja|ミネルヴァ論争|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minerva Debate|1=Minerva Debate|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109358929}}.
== Works == === Major Publications === * ''Japanese Geography'' (1899) * ''Hōryū-ji and Its Reconstruction Debate'' (1910) * ''Stories of Japanese History'' (1928) * ''Studies on Buraku Issues'' (1968)
== Legacy == Kita's pioneering studies bridged history and archaeology, influencing subsequent generations of scholars. His work on Japanese folklore and geographical history remains integral to the understanding of Japan's cultural heritage.
== References == * {{Cite book|author=Eiji Oguma|title=The Myth of a Homogeneous Nation|year=1995}} * {{Cite journal|author=Daisuke Itō|title=Archival Materials on Sadakichi Kita|journal=Tohoku Asian Studies}}
== External Links == * [http://www.example.com Biography of Sadakichi Kita]

'''Sadakichi Kita''' (喜田 貞吉, July 11, 1871 – July 3, 1939) was a pre-World War II Japanese historian and literary scholar. He integrated {{ill|Archaeology|en|Archaeology|WD=Q23498|lt=archaeology|1=Archaeology}} and {{ill|Folklore studies|en|Folklore studies|WD=Q132116|lt=folklore studies|1=Folklore studies}} into his academic research.
== Career ==
Sadakichi Kita was born into a farming family in {{ill|Kushibuchi Village, Awa Province|ja|阿波国那賀郡櫛淵村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kushibuchi Village, Awa Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (present-day {{ill|Komatsushima City, Tokushima Prefecture|ja|徳島県小松島市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Komatsushima City, Tokushima Prefecture|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}). After graduating from local schools, he advanced to {{ill|Tokushima Junior High School|ja|徳島中学校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokushima Junior High School|1=Tokushima Junior High School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q23996561}}, followed by {{ill|Third Higher School|en|Third Higher School|WD=Q11602219|lt=Third High School (Old System)|1=Third Higher School}}. In 1893, at the age of 23, he enrolled in the Faculty of Letters at {{ill|Imperial Universities|en|Imperial Universities|WD=Q562092|lt=Tokyo Imperial University|1=Imperial Universities}}, where he studied history. Among his classmates were {{ill|Ginzō Uchida|ja|内田銀蔵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ginzō Uchida|1=Ginzō Uchida|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11394369}} and {{ill|Katsumi Kuroita|ja|黒板勝美|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsumi Kuroita|1=Katsumi Kuroita|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q3894478}}. After graduating in 1896 from the Department of National History, he continued his postgraduate studies under scholars such as {{ill|Kumazo Tsuboi|ja|坪井九馬三|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumazo Tsuboi|1=Tsuboi Kumezō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11425688}}, focusing on "the historical geography of Japan."
During his time as a lecturer at the university, Kita published textbooks such as "Japanese Geography" and "Foreign Geography" (co-authored with {{ill|Shigetomo Kōda|ja|幸田成友|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shigetomo Kōda|1=Shigetomo Kōda|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q21699628}}). In 1909, he earned a {{ill|Doctorate in Literature|ja|文学博士|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Doctorate in Literature|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 博士（文学）|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 博士（文学）|1=Doctor of Letters|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q837343}} for his research on {{ill|Heijō-kyō|en|Heijō-kyō|WD=Q862509|lt=Heijō-kyō|1=Heijō-kyō}} and the {{ill|Reconstruction debate of Hōryū-ji|ja|法隆寺再建論争|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Reconstruction debate of Hōryū-ji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Later, his involvement in writing {{ill|national textbooks|ja|国定教科書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/national textbooks|1=national textbooks|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420710}} for elementary schools led to controversy. In 1911, his decision to present both the {{ill|Northern Court|en|Northern Court|WD=Q1201819|lt=Northern Court|1=Northern Court}} and {{ill|Southern Court|ja|南朝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Southern Court|qq=}} during the {{ill|Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|en|Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|WD=Q8140349|lt=Nanboku-chō period|1=Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)}} in textbooks resulted in public backlash, eventually leading to his temporary suspension.
From 1913, Kita served as a lecturer at {{ill|Kyoto Imperial University|ja|京都帝国大学|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto Imperial University|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 京都大学|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 京都大学|1=Kyoto University|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q336264}} and became a professor there in 1920. By 1924, he had joined {{ill|Tohoku Imperial University|ja|東北帝国大学|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tohoku Imperial University|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東北大学|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東北大学|1=Tohoku University|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1062129}}. There, he laid the foundation for the study of ancient Japanese history and archaeology.
== Contributions to Research ==
Kita proposed independent theories on the formation of the Japanese ethnic group and explored the history of Japan's origins through archaeological and historical studies. One notable hypothesis suggested that people creating {{ill|Jōmon pottery|en|Jōmon pottery|WD=Q4390366|lt=Jōmon pottery|1=Jōmon pottery}} continued to live in Eastern Japan until the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}}, sparking the {{ill|Minerva Debate|ja|ミネルヴァ論争|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minerva Debate|1=Minerva Debate|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q109358929}}.
== Works == === Major Publications === * ''Japanese Geography'' (1899) * ''Hōryū-ji and Its Reconstruction Debate'' (1910) * ''Stories of Japanese History'' (1928) * ''Studies on Buraku Issues'' (1968)
== Legacy == Kita's pioneering studies bridged history and archaeology, influencing subsequent generations of scholars. His work on Japanese folklore and geographical history remains integral to the understanding of Japan's cultural heritage.
== References == * {{Cite book|author=Eiji Oguma|title=The Myth of a Homogeneous Nation|year=1995}} * {{Cite journal|author=Daisuke Itō|title=Archival Materials on Sadakichi Kita|journal=Tohoku Asian Studies}}
== External Links == * [http://www.example.com Biography of Sadakichi Kita]

'''Sadakichi Kita''' (喜田 貞吉, July 11, 1871 – July 3, 1939) was a pre-World War II Japanese historian and literary scholar. He integrated {{ill|Archaeology|en|Archaeology|qid=Q23498|lt=archaeology|1=Archaeology}} and {{ill|Folklore studies|en|Folklore studies|qid=Q132116|lt=folklore studies|1=Folklore studies}} into his academic research.
== Career ==
Sadakichi Kita was born into a farming family in {{ill|Kushibuchi Village, Awa Province|ja|阿波国那賀郡櫛淵村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kushibuchi Village, Awa Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (present-day {{ill|Komatsushima City, Tokushima Prefecture|ja|徳島県小松島市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Komatsushima City, Tokushima Prefecture|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}). After graduating from local schools, he advanced to {{ill|Tokushima Junior High School|ja|徳島中学校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokushima Junior High School|1=Tokushima Junior High School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q23996561}}, followed by {{ill|Third Higher School|en|Third Higher School|qid=Q11602219|lt=Third High School (Old System)|1=Third Higher School}}. In 1893, at the age of 23, he enrolled in the Faculty of Letters at {{ill|Imperial Universities|en|Imperial Universities|qid=Q562092|lt=Tokyo Imperial University|1=Imperial Universities}}, where he studied history. Among his classmates were {{ill|Ginzō Uchida|ja|内田銀蔵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ginzō Uchida|1=Ginzō Uchida|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11394369}} and {{ill|Katsumi Kuroita|ja|黒板勝美|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsumi Kuroita|1=Katsumi Kuroita|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q3894478}}. After graduating in 1896 from the Department of National History, he continued his postgraduate studies under scholars such as {{ill|Kumazo Tsuboi|ja|坪井九馬三|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumazo Tsuboi|1=Tsuboi Kumezō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11425688}}, focusing on "the historical geography of Japan."
During his time as a lecturer at the university, Kita published textbooks such as "Japanese Geography" and "Foreign Geography" (co-authored with {{ill|Shigetomo Kōda|ja|幸田成友|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shigetomo Kōda|1=Shigetomo Kōda|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q21699628}}). In 1909, he earned a {{ill|Doctorate in Literature|ja|文学博士|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Doctorate in Literature|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 博士（文学）|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 博士（文学）|1=Doctor of Letters|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q837343}} for his research on {{ill|Heijō-kyō|en|Heijō-kyō|qid=Q862509|lt=Heijō-kyō|1=Heijō-kyō}} and the {{ill|Reconstruction debate of Hōryū-ji|ja|法隆寺再建論争|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Reconstruction debate of Hōryū-ji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Later, his involvement in writing {{ill|national textbooks|ja|国定教科書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/national textbooks|1=national textbooks|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11420710}} for elementary schools led to controversy. In 1911, his decision to present both the {{ill|Northern Court|en|Northern Court|qid=Q1201819|lt=Northern Court|1=Northern Court}} and {{ill|Southern Court|ja|南朝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Southern Court|qq=}} during the {{ill|Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|en|Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|qid=Q8140349|lt=Nanboku-chō period|1=Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)}} in textbooks resulted in public backlash, eventually leading to his temporary suspension.
From 1913, Kita served as a lecturer at {{ill|Kyoto Imperial University|ja|京都帝国大学|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto Imperial University|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 京都大学|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 京都大学|1=Kyoto University|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q336264}} and became a professor there in 1920. By 1924, he had joined {{ill|Tohoku Imperial University|ja|東北帝国大学|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tohoku Imperial University|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東北大学|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東北大学|1=Tohoku University|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1062129}}. There, he laid the foundation for the study of ancient Japanese history and archaeology.
== Contributions to Research ==
Kita proposed independent theories on the formation of the Japanese ethnic group and explored the history of Japan's origins through archaeological and historical studies. One notable hypothesis suggested that people creating {{ill|Jōmon pottery|en|Jōmon pottery|qid=Q4390366|lt=Jōmon pottery|1=Jōmon pottery}} continued to live in Eastern Japan until the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|qid=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}}, sparking the {{ill|Minerva Debate|ja|ミネルヴァ論争|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minerva Debate|1=Minerva Debate|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q109358929}}.
== Works == === Major Publications === * ''Japanese Geography'' (1899) * ''Hōryū-ji and Its Reconstruction Debate'' (1910) * ''Stories of Japanese History'' (1928) * ''Studies on Buraku Issues'' (1968)
== Legacy == Kita's pioneering studies bridged history and archaeology, influencing subsequent generations of scholars. His work on Japanese folklore and geographical history remains integral to the understanding of Japan's cultural heritage.
== References == * {{Cite book|author=Eiji Oguma|title=The Myth of a Homogeneous Nation|year=1995}} * {{Cite journal|author=Daisuke Itō|title=Archival Materials on Sadakichi Kita|journal=Tohoku Asian Studies}}
== External Links == * [http://www.example.com Biography of Sadakichi Kita]

'''Sadakichi Kita''' (喜田 貞吉, July 11, 1871 – July 3, 1939) was a pre-World War II Japanese historian and literary scholar. He integrated {{ill|Archaeology|en|Archaeology|qid=Q23498|lt=archaeology|1=Archaeology}} and {{ill|Folklore studies|en|Folklore studies|qid=Q132116|lt=folklore studies|1=Folklore studies}} into his academic research.
== Career ==
Sadakichi Kita was born into a farming family in {{ill|Kushibuchi Village, Awa Province|ja|阿波国那賀郡櫛淵村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kushibuchi Village, Awa Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (present-day {{ill|Komatsushima City, Tokushima Prefecture|ja|徳島県小松島市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Komatsushima City, Tokushima Prefecture|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}). After graduating from local schools, he advanced to {{ill|Tokushima Junior High School|ja|徳島中学校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokushima Junior High School|1=Tokushima Junior High School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q23996561}}, followed by {{ill|Third Higher School|en|Third Higher School|qid=Q11602219|lt=Third High School (Old System)|1=Third Higher School}}. In 1893, at the age of 23, he enrolled in the Faculty of Letters at {{ill|Imperial Universities|en|Imperial Universities|qid=Q562092|lt=Tokyo Imperial University|1=Imperial Universities}}, where he studied history. Among his classmates were {{ill|Ginzō Uchida|ja|内田銀蔵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ginzō Uchida|1=Ginzō Uchida|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11394369}} and {{ill|Katsumi Kuroita|ja|黒板勝美|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Katsumi Kuroita|1=Katsumi Kuroita|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q3894478}}. After graduating in 1896 from the Department of National History, he continued his postgraduate studies under scholars such as {{ill|Kumazo Tsuboi|ja|坪井九馬三|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumazo Tsuboi|1=Tsuboi Kumezō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11425688}}, focusing on "the historical geography of Japan."
During his time as a lecturer at the university, Kita published textbooks such as "Japanese Geography" and "Foreign Geography" (co-authored with {{ill|Shigetomo Kōda|ja|幸田成友|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shigetomo Kōda|1=Shigetomo Kōda|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q21699628}}). In 1909, he earned a {{ill|Doctorate in Literature|ja|文学博士|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Doctorate in Literature|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 博士（文学）|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 博士（文学）|1=Doctor of Letters|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q837343}} for his research on {{ill|Heijō-kyō|en|Heijō-kyō|qid=Q862509|lt=Heijō-kyō|1=Heijō-kyō}} and the {{ill|Reconstruction debate of Hōryū-ji|ja|法隆寺再建論争|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Reconstruction debate of Hōryū-ji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Later, his involvement in writing {{ill|national textbooks|ja|国定教科書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/national textbooks|1=national textbooks|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11420710}} for elementary schools led to controversy. In 1911, his decision to present both the {{ill|Northern Court|en|Northern Court|qid=Q1201819|lt=Northern Court|1=Northern Court}} and {{ill|Southern Court|ja|南朝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Southern Court|qq=}} during the {{ill|Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|en|Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|qid=Q8140349|lt=Nanboku-chō period|1=Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)}} in textbooks resulted in public backlash, eventually leading to his temporary suspension.
From 1913, Kita served as a lecturer at {{ill|Kyoto Imperial University|ja|京都帝国大学|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto Imperial University|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 京都大学|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 京都大学|1=Kyoto University|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q336264}} and became a professor there in 1920. By 1924, he had joined {{ill|Tohoku Imperial University|ja|東北帝国大学|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tohoku Imperial University|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東北大学|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 東北大学|1=Tohoku University|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1062129}}. There, he laid the foundation for the study of ancient Japanese history and archaeology.
== Contributions to Research ==
Kita proposed independent theories on the formation of the Japanese ethnic group and explored the history of Japan's origins through archaeological and historical studies. One notable hypothesis suggested that people creating {{ill|Jōmon pottery|en|Jōmon pottery|qid=Q4390366|lt=Jōmon pottery|1=Jōmon pottery}} continued to live in Eastern Japan until the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|qid=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}}, sparking the {{ill|Minerva Debate|ja|ミネルヴァ論争|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minerva Debate|1=Minerva Debate|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q109358929}}.
== Works == === Major Publications === * ''Japanese Geography'' (1899) * ''Hōryū-ji and Its Reconstruction Debate'' (1910) * ''Stories of Japanese History'' (1928) * ''Studies on Buraku Issues'' (1968)
== Legacy == Kita's pioneering studies bridged history and archaeology, influencing subsequent generations of scholars. His work on Japanese folklore and geographical history remains integral to the understanding of Japan's cultural heritage.
== References == * {{Cite book|author=Eiji Oguma|title=The Myth of a Homogeneous Nation|year=1995}} * {{Cite journal|author=Daisuke Itō|title=Archival Materials on Sadakichi Kita|journal=Tohoku Asian Studies}}
